topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Arthur had always found solace in miniature model shipbuilding. The precise cutting of wood, the meticulous application of paint, the quiet focus of assembling tiny galleons—it was his anchor. Then came Leo’s birthday. Leo, at seven, had discovered Arthur’s dusty workshop and, with wide, shining eyes, had completely absorbed the world of nautical craft. Now, Arthur watched as Leo, instead of playing with blocks, was dismantling old electronics to build intricate, miniature ship engines out of salvaged wires. Every time Arthur picked up his tools, a cold knot tightened in his chest. He kept glancing at the workbench, half-expecting to see a tiny, rogue mast sprouting from a circuit board. "Leo, sweetheart, those wires are very delicate," Arthur managed, his voice coming out strained, higher pitched than he intended. Leo just beamed, his concentration absolute, his small fingers moving with frightening speed. Arthur felt a sudden, desperate urge to slam the toolbox shut, to lock the door, to somehow erase the last few years of his quiet, ordered life.

[story 2]

For decades, Clara’s passion had been astronomy. She loved the vast, silent expanse of the night sky, the cold logic of celestial mechanics. Her telescope was her sanctuary. But when her son, Finn, inherited her old star charts, the quiet reverence turned into a frantic, consuming energy. Finn didn't just look at constellations; he insisted on building complex, working models of nebulae out of colored resin and tiny LED lights, claiming they needed to be 'perfectly scaled.' Last night, Clara found Finn hunched over his desk, illuminated by the harsh glow of a desk lamp, his breath catching in shallow gasps as he adjusted a miniature planetary ring. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating. Clara’s hands trembled as she reached for the lamp switch, her knuckles white against the plastic casing. "Finn," she whispered, the sound barely audible, "that needs to be put down. It’s too much." Finn didn't look up, his focus unwavering, a small, intense frown creasing his brow as he fine-tuned a minuscule star cluster.

[story 3]

Marcus had spent twenty years perfecting the art of calligraphy. The flow of the ink, the deliberate curve of each stroke—it was meditative, a slow, deliberate dance with the nib. Now, his sixteen-year-old daughter, Maya, had taken that discipline and weaponized it. Maya wasn't just writing; she was creating epic, sprawling narratives, demanding Marcus transcribe every single word, every flourish, insisting that the script needed to reflect the 'emotional weight' of the story. Today, Marcus was trying to execute a simple Latin phrase, but Maya kept leaning over his shoulder, her breath hot against his ear, murmuring corrections. "No, Dad, the serifs need more tension here. It needs to *feel* like a declaration." Marcus’s heart hammered against his ribs, a frantic rhythm entirely separate from the steady beat of his own pulse. He could feel a rising tide of sheer overwhelm washing over him. He squeezed his eyes shut for a brief second, fighting the urge to drop the inkwell, to let the carefully prepared parchment slip from his grasp.
